explicit_text: This interview is being recorded in the studios of VoicesofOklahoma.com. It's a platform where I share my story and experiences, connecting my personal history to a broader audience. Being part of this organization allows me to reflect on my life and the influences that shaped me.

explicit_text: I was born and raised in the Bronx, New York City, where I lived until 1972. Growing up there in the '50s and '60s meant living in a vibrant, diverse neighborhood with many small businesses like bakeries and grocery stores just a block away. My family lived in a fourth-floor walk-up without an elevator, and life was full of walking and subway rides to Manhattan for theater and shopping.
implicit_text: I was born and raised in a borough of New York City, where I lived until 1972. Growing up there in the '50s and '60s meant living in a vibrant, diverse neighborhood with many small businesses like bakeries and grocery stores just a block away. My family lived in a fourth-floor walk-up without an elevator, and life was full of walking and subway rides to Manhattan for theater and shopping.

explicit_text: New York City was my home from birth until I left for college in 1972. It was where my family lived through ups and downs, including my mother's early life in the city and my father's career after World War II. The city shaped my childhood with its proximity to places like Yankee Stadium and the bustling neighborhoods of the Bronx.
implicit_text: The city that never sleeps was my home from birth until I left for college in 1972. The city shaped my childhood with its proximity to places like Yankee Stadium and the bustling neighborhoods of the Bronx.

explicit_text: Phillip Lynch was my maternal grandfather, a significant figure in my family history. He was the youngest man to have a seat on the New York Stock Exchange before the Great Depression hit. His financial downfall on October 29, 1929, deeply affected my mother’s upbringing, shaping her values and our family's emphasis on education.
implicit_text: My maternal grandfather was a significant figure in my family history. He was the youngest man to have a seat on the major American stock exchange before the Great Depression hit. His financial downfall on October 29, 1929, deeply affected my mother’s upbringing, shaping her values and our family's emphasis on education.

explicit_text: The New York Stock Exchange was central to my grandfather Phillip Lynch's early success, as he was the youngest man to hold a seat there. However, the crash on October 29, 1929, wiped out his fortune, which had a lasting impact on my mother and her outlook on life and education. This event taught our family resilience and the importance of moving forward.
implicit_text: The major American stock exchange was central to my grandfather's early success, as he was the youngest man to hold a seat there. explicit_text: The Great Depression began on October 29, 1929, the day my mother turned twelve, and it changed her life dramatically. My grandfather lost everything, and my mother went from a wealthy upbringing to having to attend a free high school and work instead of going to college. Despite this hardship, she never dwelled on it and instead focused on education and moving forward.
implicit_text: The economic crisis that began on October 29, 1929, the day my mother turned twelve, changed her life dramatically. My grandfather lost everything, and my mother went from a wealthy upbringing to having to attend a free high school and work instead of going to college. Despite this hardship, she never dwelled on it and instead focused on education and moving forward.

explicit_text: Joseph Eagan was my father, a quiet and thoughtful man who spoke with purpose. After World War II, he became the general manager of a roofing supply company in New York City. Like my mother, he couldn't attend college because of the war, but he worked hard, holding three jobs to put all three of us through college.

Like my mother, he couldn't attend college because of the war, but he worked hard, holding three jobs to put all three of us through college.

explicit_text: North Adams, Massachusetts, is where my paternal grandfather was from. The Eagan family name, spelled E-A-G-A-N, was clustered there before my grandfather moved to New York City. This background connects my family roots from a small town to the big city life I experienced growing up.
implicit_text: A small city in Massachusetts is where my paternal grandfather was from. The family name, spelled E-A-G-A-N, was clustered there before my grandfather moved to New York City. This background connects my family roots from a small town to the big city life I experienced growing up.

explicit_text: Irving Berlin was a famous composer and songwriter, and my paternal grandfather worked as his chauffeur in New York City. This connection is a unique part of my family history, linking us to an iconic figure in American music. It highlights the diverse and interesting lives my family members led.

explicit_text: World War II impacted both my parents’ lives significantly. My father served and after the war became a business manager without a college degree, while my mother had to work right out of high school because of the economic conditions. The war shaped their opportunities and instilled in them a strong work ethic that they passed on to us.
implicit_text: The global conflict of the 1940s impacted both my parents’ lives significantly. The war shaped their opportunities and instilled in them a strong work ethic that they passed on to us.

explicit_text: I attended the Academy of Mount Saint Ursula, a private Catholic girls’ high school in the Bronx that my mother also attended before she had to leave due to financial hardship. It was an important part of my education and upbringing, reinforcing the Catholic values my parents emphasized. The school shaped my early academic and social life.

explicit_text: I moved to Washington, D.C. for college, attending Trinity University there. The city was a new environment for me, very different from the Bronx, and it was where I began to take charge of my academic life. Washington, D.C. also placed me near other institutions like Catholic University and Howard University, enriching my college experience.

explicit_text: Growing up in New York, I was aware of the Brooklyn Dodgers as part of the city's rich sports culture. Although I didn’t attend their games, they were part of the sports landscape alongside the Yankees and Giants during my childhood. Baseball was a big part of Saturday entertainment in our household.

explicit_text: The New York Yankees were a big part of my childhood; my brother’s high school was right across the street from Yankee Stadium. My father and I went to games together, and watching the Yankees on TV every Saturday was a family tradition. The stadium and the team were a constant presence in our lives growing up in the Bronx.

explicit_text: I attended Trinity University in Washington, D.C., which was a Catholic institution close to Catholic University and Howard University. My parents insisted on a Catholic college to maintain my faith during the turbulent 1960s. Though I struggled my freshman year, I improved and graduated cum laude with a double major in sociology and French.

explicit_text: Mount Holyoke College was my sister’s choice for me when I was looking at colleges, as it was one of the Seven Sisters and had a great academic reputation. However, my mother objected because it wasn’t a Catholic school, which was important to our family. This led me to choose Trinity University instead, which was Catholic and close to home.

explicit_text: I spent my junior year of college in Switzerland, an experience that was very important to my personal and academic growth. That year abroad helped shape my worldview and is connected to why I eventually ended up in Tulsa. It was a formative time during my undergraduate studies.
